# Story 4.1: Author `docs/RELEASING.md` with six-scenario rollback playbook

Status: ready-for-dev

<!-- Note: Validation is optional. Run validate-create-story for quality check before dev-story. -->

## Story

As the maintainer (or a future contributor with release access),
I want the `## Rollback Playbook` placeholder at `docs/RELEASING.md:181-185` expanded into a full six-scenario rollback playbook (A–F) with concrete CLI commands, plus a seventh scenario (G) for "release.yaml disabled/reverted/missing" — each grounded in the live evidence from Story 3.2's alpha cut (`bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1-alpha.0`, run `24714953668`, tag `v0.10.1-alpha.0`) and using name-based `gh api` lookups instead of hard-coded ruleset/env IDs,
so that when a release fails or ships broken, I can execute a known-good recovery path under pressure instead of improvising against npm's one-shot semantics, and so that Story 5.1's pre-v1.0.0 readiness audit has its AC #1 precondition ("`docs/RELEASING.md` rollback playbook exists") met.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. **Rollback Playbook placeholder replaced with full content.** The stub block in `docs/RELEASING.md` (the `<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->` comment + the `## Rollback Playbook` heading + the `_To be authored..._` italic placeholder paragraph — at lines 181-185 as of 2026-04-21; re-verify at Task 1 recon in case `main` has drifted) SHALL be replaced with a complete, operational rollback playbook section. Post-patch, `grep -n 'To be authored' docs/RELEASING.md` SHALL return empty, and `grep -cE '^(## Rollback Playbook$|### Scenario [A-G])' docs/RELEASING.md` SHALL return `8` (one `## Rollback Playbook` header + seven scenario sub-headings A–G).

2. **Immutability banner at section top** (NFR6, FR14). Immediately under the `## Rollback Playbook` heading, BEFORE Scenario A, a prominent callout (blockquote or bold paragraph) SHALL state verbatim or equivalent: **"NEVER `npm unpublish` v1.0.0 — once published under `--tag latest`, the version is immutable by policy. The default rollback is `npm deprecate` + ship forward. See Scenario C for the narrow 72h/zero-dependents exception that still applies to pre-v1.0.0 versions only."** The banner SHALL NOT be hidden inside a scenario; it is a section-level constraint.

3. **Scenario A — "Bad version published to `latest`, no users yet (within ~minutes)"** (research doc lines 927-937 baseline + Story 3.2 evidence). Documented with: (a) **trigger condition** — maintainer notices defect within minutes of publish; `npm view <pkg> dist-tags` shows bad version on `latest`; download count negligible. (b) **Concrete CLI sequence** — `npm dist-tag add bmad-module-skill-forge@<previous_good> latest` → `npm dist-tag ls bmad-module-skill-forge` (verify) → `npm deprecate bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> "Pulled - use <previous_good> instead"` → dispatch `release.yaml` with `version_bump: patch` for the fix. (c) **Expected outcome** — `latest` now points at `<previous_good>`; `@<bad>` shows deprecation warning on install; subsequent patch cuts via the canonical workflow. (d) **Constraints** — only valid while the blast radius is small; if any downstream has pinned to `<bad>`, Scenario B applies instead. (e) **Verification command** — `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge dist-tags --json` should return `{"latest":"<previous_good>", ...}`.

4. **Scenario B — "Bad version live for hours/days, users may be installing it"** (research doc lines 939-945 baseline). Documented with: (a) **trigger** — defect discovered after `<bad>` has been `latest` long enough that download count is non-zero or dependents may have pinned. (b) **CLI** — `npm deprecate bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> "Critical bug - use <next_version> instead"` IMMEDIATELY (warns on every install); cut fixed patch via `release.yaml` with `version_bump: patch`; the workflow's dist-tag case-chain auto-updates `latest` for non-prerelease versions. (c) **Expected outcome** — `npm install bmad-module-skill-forge` now emits the deprecation warning; new patch publishes cleanly; `latest` advances. (d) **Constraints** — DO NOT `npm dist-tag add @previous_good latest` here; for post-v1.0.0 that is a NFR6 violation, and for pre-v1.0.0 it fragments user expectation. Ship forward via patch bump. (e) **Cross-reference** — NFR3 rollback-frequency target (<1/quarter post-v1.0.0) is evaluated against the frequency of Scenario B invocations.

5. **Scenario C — "Bad version within last 72h, zero downloads, zero dependents"** (research doc lines 947-953 baseline). Documented with: (a) **trigger** — narrow eligibility window per [npm unpublish policy](https://docs.npmjs.com/policies/unpublish/): less than 72 hours since publish AND zero downloads AND zero dependent packages on npmjs.com. (b) **CLI** — `npm unpublish bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad>` — this is the only path to actual removal. (c) **Expected outcome** — version disappears from `npm view`; however the version **number** is permanently burned (npm rejects re-publish under the same version forever). (d) **Constraints** — explicit warning: "The version number is permanently burned. The next release MUST bump past `<bad>` — `npm version patch` on `0.10.1` after unpublishing `0.10.1` will next produce `0.10.2`, not `0.10.1` again." Plus: "This scenario does NOT apply to v1.0.0 — per NFR6, v1.0.0 is never unpublished regardless of eligibility window. Use Scenario B instead." (e) **Verification** — `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<bad> 2>&1` should return `404 No match found`.

6. **Scenario D — "Tag exists but npm publish failed"** (research doc lines 955-961 baseline + Story 3.2 evidence expansion). Documented with: (a) **trigger** — `release.yaml` tagged + pushed the `v*` tag in the `Create and push tag` step (line 161-167 of `release.yaml`), then the `Publish to npm via OIDC trusted publishing` step (line 181) failed (OIDC 404, tarball validation error, network). (b) **Concrete CLI** — `git tag -d v<version>` (local) → `git push --delete origin v<version>` (remote) → re-dispatch `release.yaml` with the same `version_bump` input. (c) **Expected outcome** — tag cleared from origin; re-run produces a fresh clean tag + successful publish. (d) **Constraints** — this is ONLY safe if publish failed; if npm DID publish, Scenario E applies (the tag-delete path would orphan the npm artifact). Always verify publish state FIRST with `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<version> 2>&1` before running `git push --delete`. (e) **Story 3.2 load-bearing context** — the current `release.yaml` pushes the tag BEFORE publish (line 161-167 vs. line 181). This ordering is pre-existing from Story 3.1 and tracked in `deferred-work.md § "Create and push tag pushes the git tag BEFORE Publish to npm"` as a post-v1.0.0 hardening candidate; until then, Scenario D's tag-delete path IS the recovery. (f) **Do-NOT clause** — NEVER re-use the burned `<version>` number; `release.yaml` will produce `<version>+1` on the next dispatch (e.g. `0.10.1-alpha.0` re-runs produce `0.10.1-alpha.1`, not `0.10.1-alpha.0` again; that would require `npm version <exact> --no-git-tag-version` override which is out of scope for rollback).

7. **Scenario E — "npm publish succeeded but GitHub Release / tag push failed"** (research doc lines 963-971 baseline + Story 3.2 inverted-premise correction). Documented with: (a) **trigger** — publish succeeded (`npm view @<version>` returns manifest), but one of: tag-push to origin failed (network; branch protection edge case); `Create GitHub Release` softprops step failed (API rate limit; auth expiry); `Push commit to main` skipped as-designed but then release artifact shape doesn't match main. (b) **Premise inversion note** — Story 3.2 observed ordering: `Create and push tag` runs BEFORE `Publish to npm via OIDC`. In the current `release.yaml` this means tag push is the more likely failure-point vs. publish, reversing the research-doc narrative. The Scenario E playbook MUST cover the **post-publish-tag-missing** recovery (tag push failed AFTER publish succeeded) — which is a small window but real. (c) **CLI for tag recovery** — `git tag -a v<version> <commit-sha> -m "Release v<version>"` → `git push origin v<version>`. Obtain `<commit-sha>` from the workflow run's `Commit version bump` step log or from `gh run view <run-id> --log | grep -A 2 "release: bump to v<version>"`. (d) **CLI for GH Release recovery** — `gh release create v<version> --notes-file release_notes.md --title "Skill Forge (SKF) v<version>"` using the `release_notes.md` produced in the run's `Generate release notes` step (scrape from `gh run view <run-id> --log` if not preserved). (e) **Constraints** — npm state is immutable; DO NOT try to "clean up" the npm artifact to re-run the release. The npm artifact + orphaned post-recovery git state is the canonical record. (f) **Orphaned-commit caveat (Story 3.2 context)** — if the run was dispatched from a feature branch and `Push commit to main` was correctly skipped, the `release: bump to v<version>` commit lives only in the run log or tagged via the recovered tag above. This is NFR12-compliant and expected behavior; the tag is authoritative.

8. **Scenario F — "Suspected OIDC compromise / unauthorized publish"** (research doc lines 973-977 baseline + Story 1.3 TP registration surface + Story 3.2 provenance verification). Documented with: (a) **trigger** — `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge time` shows a publish with a timestamp no maintainer triggered; OR `npm audit signatures` on a published tarball reports attestation verification failure; OR a GitHub Actions run is visible with reviewer approval that was not given; OR npm support notifies of suspected compromise. (b) **Immediate CLI (within minutes):** visit `https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge` → Settings tab → **delete the Trusted Publisher entry** (revoke OIDC trust); this blocks all future OIDC publishes until re-registered. (c) **Audit CLI** — `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge time` (lists all publishes with timestamps); `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@<suspect> --json | jq '.dist.attestations'` (should show `predicateType: https://slsa.dev/provenance/v1` + valid attestation URL — if the attestation is missing or the `url` field is unexpected, the publish bypassed OIDC); `npm audit signatures` on a fresh install of each suspect version (expected: "X packages have verified attestations"). (d) **Coordination CLI** — if a malicious version was published, contact `support@npmjs.com` with the version string + attestation URL + Trusted Publisher registration timestamp; request manual takedown (npm support CAN unpublish even outside the 72h window for security-compromise cases). (e) **Lockdown CLI** — rotate or audit: `gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/secrets` (confirm no unexpected secrets); `gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/collaborators` (confirm write-access list unchanged); review `.github/workflows/*.yaml` for any `pull_request: types: [opened]` trigger that could run attacker-controlled code with repo secrets. (f) **Post-incident reactivation** — after audit completes and root cause is identified + patched, re-register Trusted Publisher via the npm UI with the four fields matching `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher`; re-verify via an alpha cut before any stable release. (g) **Do-NOT clause** — DO NOT rotate `NPM_TOKEN` as a first response; the token is not the OIDC path and rotating it does nothing to stop an OIDC compromise. If the token itself is also suspected compromised, revoke it via npmjs.com → Access Tokens and remove from repo secrets; but this is separate from OIDC incident response. (h) **Pre-v1.0.0 context** — Trusted Publisher was pre-registered on 2026-04-20 (Story 1.3). A compromise discovered pre-v1.0.0 is recoverable via delete-and-re-register (no destructive side-effect since no production user base); post-v1.0.0 the incident has downstream-consumer blast radius and requires the support@npmjs.com coordination path.

9. **Scenario G — "release.yaml disabled, reverted, or missing from main"** (new — Epic 3 retro commitment 2; deferred-work `§ 3-3 code review "No end-to-end assertion that release.yaml owns the v* tag-push path"`). Documented with: (a) **trigger** — `release.yaml` is the single-rooted release workflow (Patch A in Story 3.3 neutralized `publish.yaml`'s `v*` tag trigger); if `release.yaml` is reverted, renamed, or disabled (repo-settings-level "Disable workflow") but a `v*` tag push occurs, NO release fires and nobody notices until a maintainer checks `npm view`. (b) **Detection** — cadence check: after any PR touching `.github/workflows/*` and before any release attempt, run `gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/workflows --jq '.workflows[] | select(.name=="Release") | {name, state, path}'` → expected `{name: "Release", state: "active", path: ".github/workflows/release.yaml"}`. If `state: disabled_manually` OR no matching workflow returned, Scenario G applies. (c) **Recovery CLI** — `gh workflow enable release.yaml` (re-enables disabled workflow); if the file is missing, revert the offending commit via `gh pr revert <pr-number>` (which opens a revert PR through normal branch-protection review). Do NOT hand-edit `main` — branch protection blocks direct push anyway. (d) **Prevention** — Story 3.3 AC #9's no-double-publish grep evidence is the baseline: `grep -l 'id-token: write' .github/workflows/*.yaml` should list `docs.yaml` (GitHub Pages — orthogonal), `publish.yaml` (DEPRECATED, workflow_dispatch-only), `release.yaml` (canonical). `grep -r 'tags:' .github/workflows/*.yaml` should return zero matches on any `v*` pattern outside of `release.yaml` (which has no `push: tags:` trigger either — it's `workflow_dispatch` only). (e) **Escalation path** — if Scenario G is detected in the middle of an incident where a release is urgently needed, the legacy `publish.yaml` retains `workflow_dispatch:` as an emergency hatch (NPM_TOKEN still at repo scope until Story 6.3); invoke via `gh workflow run publish.yaml` with the explicit understanding that the publish will be token-based (NOT OIDC) and will NOT carry SLSA-L2 provenance. Document the fallback use in an incident post-mortem; re-enable `release.yaml` before the next release.

10. **Scenario cross-reference matrix.** At the end of the Rollback Playbook section, a compact table SHALL summarize: scenario letter → trigger class → primary CLI verb → NFR linkage. Format:
    ```
    | Scenario | Trigger class | Primary CLI verb | NFR linkage |
    |----------|---------------|------------------|-------------|
    | A | Fresh bad latest | dist-tag + deprecate | NFR3 |
    | B | Stale bad latest | deprecate + ship forward | NFR3, NFR6 |
    | C | Eligible unpublish | unpublish | (pre-v1.0.0 only) |
    | D | Tag orphan (publish failed) | tag -d + push --delete | NFR12 |
    | E | Post-publish state drift | tag -a + gh release create | NFR5 |
    | F | OIDC compromise | revoke TP + audit | NFR7 |
    | G | release.yaml disabled/missing | workflow enable / pr revert | NFR5, NFR10 |
    ```
    This turns prose scenarios into a scannable reference for under-pressure triage.

11. **Inspect-current-state CLI block uses NAME-BASED lookups, not hard-coded IDs** (Epic 3 retro commitment 4; deferred-work `§ 1-2 code review "Hard-coded ruleset id 13855503 and environment id 14347249917"`). Any new `gh api` command in the Rollback Playbook section that references the default-branch ruleset or the `release` environment SHALL derive the id dynamically, not embed it literally. Examples:

    - **Environment lookup:** the `release` environment is accessed by NAME (not id) in the existing `gh api repos/.../environments/release` calls, so this is already name-based — but any new CLI that drops to the environment-id API (e.g., `/environments/{environment_id}`) SHALL use `ENV_ID=$(gh api repos/.../environments/release --jq .id)` first.
    - **Rationale:** ruleset id `13855503` and environment id `14347249917` can be re-created (delete + re-add); IDs change, names are stable. Existing hard-coded IDs in the OTHER sections of `docs/RELEASING.md` (lines 14, 47, 62, 72) SHALL NOT be modified by Story 4.1 — that cleanup is a separate pass tracked in `deferred-work.md`; Story 4.1's scope is additive (new Rollback Playbook content only).

12. **Baseline capture procedure documented** (deferred-work `§ 1-2 code review "Baseline.json capture procedure unspecified"`). Within Scenario D or as a new `### Baseline snapshots` subsection under `## Rollback Playbook`, document the ONE-TIME capture pattern for the restore snippet at `docs/RELEASING.md:52-64`. Concretely:
    ```bash
    # Capture current ruleset state as a disaster-recovery baseline
    gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/$(gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id') > _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/baseline-ruleset-Default-$(date +%Y%m%d).json
    ```
    And analogously for the `release` environment. The baselines live in `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/` (already in git), NOT in `docs/` (not to be shipped with the package). Include a note: "Capture a fresh baseline after any maintainer-initiated change to the ruleset or environment — not on a schedule."

13. **Ruleset-deletion recovery path** (deferred-work `§ 1-1 code review "Restore snippet covers ruleset-edit recovery but not ruleset-deletion"`). Scenario D's CLI block (or a new sibling) SHALL cover the case where the `Default` ruleset has been **deleted** (not just edited). The existing restore snippet at `docs/RELEASING.md:52-64` uses `gh api --method PUT .../rulesets/13855503` — which returns 404 if the ruleset was deleted. Recovery path:
    ```bash
    # If PUT returns 404, recreate via POST:
    gh api --method POST repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets \
      --input _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/baseline-ruleset-Default-YYYYMMDD.json
    ```
    This requires the `name`, `target`, `enforcement`, `conditions`, `rules`, `bypass_actors` fields — all captured in the baseline per AC #12. Document the PUT-then-POST fallback as the canonical flow: "Try PUT first; if 404, POST recreates with the same fields."

## Dev Notes

### What this story actually is

A **focused doc expansion**, not a rewrite. The current `docs/RELEASING.md` already has a disciplined structure (Branch Protection → Release Environment → npm Trusted Publisher) with placeholder at `## Rollback Playbook`. Story 4.1 fills the placeholder with seven scenarios (A–F from research doc baseline + G added per Epic 3 retro commitment), each five-element-structured (trigger / CLI / outcome / constraints / verification), plus a cross-reference matrix, plus a `### Baseline snapshots` subsection, plus a small README.md link update.

Target section size: ~200-300 lines expansion to `docs/RELEASING.md`. No workflow changes (Epic 4 is doc-only per `epics.md`). No package.json / CONTRIBUTING.md / CHANGELOG.md touches (Story 4.2 / 5.1 / release.yaml territory).

**Scope discipline — what NOT to do here:**

- Do NOT add workflow hardening content (F1–F6 from Epic 3 retro). Those are post-v1.0.0 or Story 5.1 audit territory. The Rollback Playbook is a **recovery** doc, not a prevention doc.
- Do NOT add the one-time `v1.0.0-rc.1` hand-bump procedure (Epic 3 retro H8). That belongs in a new `### Cutting v1.0.0-rc.1` section owned by Story 5.1.
- Do NOT modify the existing hard-coded ruleset/env IDs at `docs/RELEASING.md:14, 47, 62, 72` (Epic 3 retro commitment 4 says NEW content uses name-based lookups; existing content cleanup is a separate pass tracked in `deferred-work.md`).
- Do NOT add CONTRIBUTING.md content or `release:*` script prefixes — Story 4.2's scope.
- Do NOT flip the DRAFT banner on STABILITY.md or any other doc — Story 5.1's scope.
- Do NOT add Starlight sidebar entries — Story 5.1's scope (shared with STABILITY.md sidebar entry deferral).
- Do NOT link to Story 5.2 / 5.3 / 5.4 / Epic 6 work in the Rollback Playbook prose — those stories haven't authored their content yet; forward-linking creates stale prose. Cross-reference only AC-mandated items from existing docs.
- Do NOT author Scenario G's "release.yaml disabled" incident playbook against hypothetical disable-workflow UI behavior you haven't verified with `gh`. Task 1 recon validates the CLI shape; base prose on observed output.

2	title: Releasing SKF
3	description: Maintainer reference for the release pipeline — branch protection, release workflow, and rollback procedures.
4	---
5	
6	This document records the configuration that gates releases of `bmad-module-skill-forge`. It exists so a future maintainer (including future-you) can audit, restore, or extend the pipeline without reverse-engineering GitHub settings.
7	
8	For background on GitHub rulesets vs legacy branch protection, see the [GitHub ruleset docs](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-rulesets/about-rulesets).
9	
10	## Branch Protection on `main`
11	
12	`main` is gated by a **GitHub repository ruleset** (not legacy branch protection). The legacy "Settings → Branches → Branch protection rules" surface returns 404 for this repo.
13	
14	**Ruleset:** `Default` — id `13855503` — applies to `~DEFAULT_BRANCH` (currently `main`) — enforcement `active`.
15	
16	**Active rules (5):**
17	
18	| Rule                     | Effect                                                                                                                     |
19	| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
20	| `deletion`               | Branch cannot be deleted.                                                                                                  |
21	| `non_fast_forward`       | Force-push blocked.                                                                                                        |
22	| `pull_request`           | Requires ≥ 1 approving review; `require_code_owner_review: true` (see CODEOWNERS note below); merge/squash/rebase allowed. |
23	| `code_quality`           | Blocks merge on `severity: errors` from GitHub code-quality checks.                                                        |
24	| `required_status_checks` | Merge blocked until all seven `quality.yaml` checks pass (names below).                                                    |
25	
26	**Required status checks (7):** sourced from `.github/workflows/quality.yaml` job keys. Matrix jobs expand to `jobname (matrix-value)`:
27	
28	- `prettier`
29	- `eslint`
30	- `markdownlint`
31	- `validate (ubuntu-latest)`
32	- `validate (windows-latest)`
33	- `python (ubuntu-latest)`
34	- `python (windows-latest)`
35	
36	**Coupling with `quality.yaml`:** if that workflow renames a job or changes the `strategy.matrix.os` for `validate` or `python`, the ruleset's `required_status_checks` list must be updated in lock-step — otherwise merges to `main` will either block on a check name that no longer reports, or silently pass without the renamed check. Update both in the same PR.
37	
38	**`strict_required_status_checks_policy: false`** — PR branches are not forced to be up-to-date with `main` before merging. This avoids constant rebases on a low-traffic repo. Flip to `true` if concurrent merges start producing logical conflicts the checks can't catch.
39	
40	**CODEOWNERS note:** the `pull_request` rule has `require_code_owner_review: true`, but no `.github/CODEOWNERS` file exists in the repo today. GitHub treats the code-owner requirement as vacuously satisfied when the file is absent, so this setting is currently a no-op — the only active review gate is `required_approving_review_count: 1`. If a CODEOWNERS file is added later, make sure the listed owners can actually approve PRs from other authors. GitHub's universal rule is that a PR author cannot approve their own PR — so a CODEOWNERS file that lists only a solo maintainer would deadlock every PR that maintainer opens (they'd be the sole eligible code-owner reviewer but also the author).
41	
42	**Bypass actors:** `RepositoryRole` actor_id=5 (Admin), `bypass_mode: pull_request`. Admins can bypass the ruleset **only via a pull request**, never via direct push. This preserves `non_fast_forward` and the required-checks gate for the `github-actions[bot]` account that the future `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1) will use to push tags and commits to `main`. **Do not add a bot-specific bypass**; it would defeat the whole purpose of this ruleset.
43	
44	### Inspect current state
45	
46	```bash
47	gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/13855503
48	```
49	
50	### Restore from a saved baseline
51	
52	A JSON baseline captured before any change can be replayed via:
53	
54	```bash
55	# Extract the full set of fields required by a ruleset PUT from a saved baseline.json.
56	# PUT replaces the resource wholesale — omitting any of these fields either 422s or
57	# silently resets them server-side. `bypass_actors` is optional (empty array is the
58	# default) but is included here to preserve the admin-via-PR bypass.
59	jq '{name, target, enforcement, conditions, rules, bypass_actors}' baseline.json > /tmp/restore.json
60	
61	gh api --method PUT \
62	  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/13855503 \
63	  --input /tmp/restore.json
64	```
65	
66	The GitHub ruleset API uses `PUT` (not `PATCH`) for updates, and the `rules` array is replaced wholesale — it is not merged server-side. Always fetch current state, modify the in-memory copy, and `PUT` the complete list.
67

70	The publish job is gated by a **GitHub [deployment environment](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/targeting-different-environments/using-environments-for-deployment) with a required-reviewer rule** (not by workflow logic). A job declaring `environment: release` pauses until a listed reviewer clicks "Approve and deploy" in the Actions UI.
71	
72	**Environment:** `release` — id `14347249917` — created 2026-04-20.
73	
74	| Setting                    | Value                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
75	| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
76	| `wait_timer`               | `0` (no artificial delay; approval is the only gate)                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
77	| `prevent_self_review`      | `false` — see rationale below                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
78	| `reviewers`                | `armelhbobdad` (user id `132626034`), 1 approver                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
79	| `deployment_branch_policy` | `custom_branch_policies: true`, list: `main` only                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
80	| Environment-scoped secrets | `0` (invariant — see `NPM_TOKEN` note below)                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
81	| Cost                       | `$0` on public-repo tier (environments, required reviewers, and branch policies are [free for public repositories](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/targeting-different-environments/using-environments-for-deployment#about-environments)) |
82	
83	**`prevent_self_review: false` — correctness constraint, not a loosened control.** Solo-maintainer setups cannot self-approve when this is `true`, so the gate would deadlock on any maintainer-triggered publish. The value flips to `true` the moment a second reviewer joins — do not leave it loose by inertia.
84	
85	**`NPM_TOKEN` is NOT scoped to this environment.** It remains at repo-level secrets during the OIDC transition and is removed entirely post-v1.0.0 (see Story 6.3). The invariant: the `release` env must have zero secrets. If a future change scopes any secret here, re-audit whether the OIDC trusted-publisher path is still in force.
86	
87	**Coupling with npm trusted publishing (Story 1.3).** The npm trusted publisher binds on four fields — `organization=armelhbobdad`, `repository=bmad-module-skill-forge`, `workflow filename=release.yaml`, `environment=release`. The environment name above is load-bearing: any rename here must be accompanied by a matching npm-side update in the same change, or the next publish returns 404.
88	
89	### Inspect current state
90	
91	```bash
92	# Environment itself (reviewers, branch-policy shape, prevent_self_review)
93	gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release
94	
95	# Allowed deployment branches (expect: one entry, name "main")
96	gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies
97	
98	# Environment-scoped secrets (expect: zero)
99	gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/secrets
100	```
101	
102	### Restore / re-apply
103	
104	Two-call pattern — the environment and its branch-policy list are separate resources. Creating the environment alone with `custom_branch_policies: true` leaves the allow-list empty, which rejects every dispatch; the second call is mandatory.
105	
106	```bash
107	# (1) Create or update the environment with the required-reviewer gate
108	gh api --method PUT repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release --input - <<'JSON'
109	{
110	  "wait_timer": 0,
111	  "prevent_self_review": false,
112	  "reviewers": [{ "type": "User", "id": 132626034 }],
113	  "deployment_branch_policy": {
114	    "protected_branches": false,
115	    "custom_branch_policies": true
116	  }
117	}
118	JSON
119	
120	# (2) Add main to the allowed deployment branches.
121	#     Returns HTTP 422 ("already_exists") if main is already on the list —
122	#     safe to ignore on re-apply. To pre-check:
123	#     gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies
124	gh api --method POST repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies --input - <<'JSON'
125	{ "name": "main" }
126	JSON
127	```
128	
129	### Temporarily allowing a feature branch
130	
131	A release workflow that declares `environment: release` will be rejected from any branch not on the allow-list. For legitimate validation cuts from a feature branch (Story 3.2's alpha cut is the canonical case), widen the allow-list for the duration of the test and tighten it back immediately:
132	
133	```bash
134	# Allow the feature branch and capture the returned policy id into a shell var.
135	# Using command substitution + --jq .id avoids the "eyeball the JSON and paste
136	# the id later" footgun — if the POST succeeds, $POLICY_ID is ready for the revoke.
137	POLICY_ID=$(gh api --method POST \
138	  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies \
139	  --input - --jq .id <<'JSON'
140	{ "name": "feat/my-validation-branch" }
141	JSON
142	)
143	echo "POLICY_ID=$POLICY_ID"  # confirm a numeric id was captured before proceeding
144	
145	# ... run the validation cut, approve via the reviewer UI, confirm publish ...
146	
147	# Revoke immediately — the allow-list must return to { main } before leaving the session.
148	# Quote the expansion so the shell never interprets the id as a redirection token.
149	gh api --method DELETE \
150	  "repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies/$POLICY_ID"
151	```
152	
153	Leaving a feature branch on the allow-list is an unguarded hole — any later `workflow_dispatch` from that branch would be able to reach the publish path. Treat the revoke as the last step of the validation, not a follow-up.
154	
155	## npm Trusted Publisher
156	
157	The future `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1) publishes to npm via **OIDC trusted publishing** — no `NPM_TOKEN` is consulted during the publish step, and every published version carries an auto-attached SLSA Build Level 2 provenance attestation. For this to work, the npm package `bmad-module-skill-forge` has a trusted-publisher entry on npmjs.com that binds on four fields exactly matching what the workflow asserts at run time. A mismatch on any field causes an opaque `404` at publish time — the error ("npm could not match your workflow run") surfaces the failure class but does not name which of the four fields is wrong.
158	
159	**Registered:** 2026-04-20 by `armelhbobdad`.
160	
161	| Field             | Value                     |
162	| ----------------- | ------------------------- |
163	| Publisher type    | `GitHub Actions`          |
164	| Organization/user | `armelhbobdad`            |
165	| Repository        | `bmad-module-skill-forge` |
166	| Workflow filename | `release.yaml`            |
167	| Environment       | `release`                 |
168	
169	**Inspect current state:** visit the [npm package settings for `bmad-module-skill-forge`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge) → **Settings** tab → **Trusted Publisher** section (npm UI as of 2026-04-20; if the tab is reorganised later, the section still lives on the package Settings page). Modification requires 2FA re-entry on the maintainer account. No CLI or public API for programmatic inspection of Trusted Publisher state exists as of 2026-04-20 — drift detection is UI-only until npm exposes one.
170	
171	**Case-sensitive.** All four fields above use the exact lowercase forms shown; npm's matcher is an exact-string comparison. Do not capitalize on re-registration even if GitHub's UI surfaces a display-form with capitals.
172	
173	**Rename coupling — the four fields are load-bearing.** Renaming OR deleting the `release` GitHub environment (see § Release Environment), renaming or moving `release.yaml` within `.github/workflows/`, or flipping the extension between `.yaml` and `.yml` each require matching updates in the same PR to: (a) the npm-side Trusted Publisher, (b) the Registered table above, and (c) the `## Release Process` enumeration in `README.md` (which names both `release` and the Trusted Publisher). Skipping any of these produces an opaque `404` on the next publish — the error names the failure class, not the specific field.
174	
175	**Pre-registration inversion.** This entry was registered **before** `release.yaml` was authored (Story 3.1). The first live validator of the full OIDC chain is Story 3.2's alpha cut. If that cut's publish step 404s, open a **three-way comparison**: (1) the npm Settings tab, (2) the workflow YAML's `name` / `on` / `jobs.<id>.environment` lines, and (3) the Registered table above. The table is the ground truth because it captured the values at npm-save time — compare both the npm record and the workflow header against the table, never the workflow against itself (verifying the workflow against its own header will silently confirm a typo).
176	
177	**`NPM_TOKEN` is a legacy residual, not a safety net.** The token remains at repo-level secrets for two reasons: (a) the legacy `publish.yaml` still uses it until Story 3.3 retires that path, and (b) Story 6.3 deletes the secret entirely post-v1.0.0. Treat its continued presence as attack surface to minimize, not defence-in-depth — a repo-scope token is reachable from any workflow with `secrets.*` access. The token does NOT sit "behind" OIDC: `release.yaml` does not yet exist, so there is no OIDC path for it to be a fallback to. If a future OIDC incident forces a last-resort token-based re-publish, document the flip in the commit body and revert as soon as OIDC is restored.
178	
179	**Fixing a bad registration.** The npm UI exposes both **Edit** and **Delete** on an existing Trusted Publisher entry (observed 2026-04-20). Prefer edit for a single-field typo; prefer delete-and-re-add if multiple fields are wrong or the edit form ever feels ambiguous. **Pre-Story 3.2**: there is no destructive side effect because no publish is attempted yet, and delete-and-re-add keeps the audit trail cleaner. **Post-Story 3.2**: a publish that fires during the delete-and-re-add window will 404 — gate any delete-and-re-add behind a manual publish freeze (pause any active `release.yaml` runs, confirm no tags are in-flight) before touching the entry.
180	
181	<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->
182	
183	## Rollback Playbook
184	
185	_To be authored in Story 4.1 — rollback playbook covering scenarios such as failed publish, tag mismatch, bad `latest` dist-tag, OIDC auth failure, and post-publish security issue._

---
title: Releasing SKF
description: Maintainer reference for the release pipeline — branch protection, release workflow, and rollback procedures.
---

This document records the configuration that gates releases of `bmad-module-skill-forge`. It exists so a future maintainer (including future-you) can audit, restore, or extend the pipeline without reverse-engineering GitHub settings.

For background on GitHub rulesets vs legacy branch protection, see the [GitHub ruleset docs](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-rulesets/about-rulesets).

## Branch Protection on `main`

`main` is gated by a **GitHub repository ruleset** (not legacy branch protection). The legacy "Settings → Branches → Branch protection rules" surface returns 404 for this repo.

**Ruleset:** `Default` — id `13855503` — applies to `~DEFAULT_BRANCH` (currently `main`) — enforcement `active`.

**Active rules (5):**

| Rule                     | Effect                                                                                                                     |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `deletion`               | Branch cannot be deleted.                                                                                                  |
| `non_fast_forward`       | Force-push blocked.                                                                                                        |
| `pull_request`           | Requires ≥ 1 approving review; `require_code_owner_review: true` (see CODEOWNERS note below); merge/squash/rebase allowed. |
| `code_quality`           | Blocks merge on `severity: errors` from GitHub code-quality checks.                                                        |
| `required_status_checks` | Merge blocked until all seven `quality.yaml` checks pass (names below).                                                    |

**Required status checks (7):** sourced from `.github/workflows/quality.yaml` job keys. Matrix jobs expand to `jobname (matrix-value)`:

- `prettier`
- `eslint`
- `markdownlint`
- `validate (ubuntu-latest)`
- `validate (windows-latest)`
- `python (ubuntu-latest)`
- `python (windows-latest)`

**Coupling with `quality.yaml`:** if that workflow renames a job or changes the `strategy.matrix.os` for `validate` or `python`, the ruleset's `required_status_checks` list must be updated in lock-step — otherwise merges to `main` will either block on a check name that no longer reports, or silently pass without the renamed check. Update both in the same PR.

**`strict_required_status_checks_policy: false`** — PR branches are not forced to be up-to-date with `main` before merging. This avoids constant rebases on a low-traffic repo. Flip to `true` if concurrent merges start producing logical conflicts the checks can't catch.

**CODEOWNERS note:** the `pull_request` rule has `require_code_owner_review: true`, but no `.github/CODEOWNERS` file exists in the repo today. GitHub treats the code-owner requirement as vacuously satisfied when the file is absent, so this setting is currently a no-op — the only active review gate is `required_approving_review_count: 1`. If a CODEOWNERS file is added later, make sure the listed owners can actually approve PRs from other authors. GitHub's universal rule is that a PR author cannot approve their own PR — so a CODEOWNERS file that lists only a solo maintainer would deadlock every PR that maintainer opens (they'd be the sole eligible code-owner reviewer but also the author).

**Bypass actors:** `RepositoryRole` actor_id=5 (Admin), `bypass_mode: pull_request`. Admins can bypass the ruleset **only via a pull request**, never via direct push. This preserves `non_fast_forward` and the required-checks gate for the `github-actions[bot]` account that the future `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1) will use to push tags and commits to `main`. **Do not add a bot-specific bypass**; it would defeat the whole purpose of this ruleset.

### Inspect current state

```bash
gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/13855503
```

### Restore from a saved baseline

A JSON baseline captured before any change can be replayed via:

```bash
# Extract the full set of fields required by a ruleset PUT from a saved baseline.json.
# PUT replaces the resource wholesale — omitting any of these fields either 422s or
# silently resets them server-side. `bypass_actors` is optional (empty array is the
# default) but is included here to preserve the admin-via-PR bypass.
jq '{name, target, enforcement, conditions, rules, bypass_actors}' baseline.json > /tmp/restore.json

gh api --method PUT \
  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/13855503 \
  --input /tmp/restore.json
```

The GitHub ruleset API uses `PUT` (not `PATCH`) for updates, and the `rules` array is replaced wholesale — it is not merged server-side. Always fetch current state, modify the in-memory copy, and `PUT` the complete list.

## Release Environment

The publish job is gated by a **GitHub [deployment environment](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/targeting-different-environments/using-environments-for-deployment) with a required-reviewer rule** (not by workflow logic). A job declaring `environment: release` pauses until a listed reviewer clicks "Approve and deploy" in the Actions UI.

**Environment:** `release` — id `14347249917` — created 2026-04-20.

| Setting                    | Value                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `wait_timer`               | `0` (no artificial delay; approval is the only gate)                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| `prevent_self_review`      | `false` — see rationale below                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| `reviewers`                | `armelhbobdad` (user id `132626034`), 1 approver                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| `deployment_branch_policy` | `custom_branch_policies: true`, list: `main` only                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| Environment-scoped secrets | `0` (invariant — see `NPM_TOKEN` note below)                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| Cost                       | `$0` on public-repo tier (environments, required reviewers, and branch policies are [free for public repositories](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/targeting-different-environments/using-environments-for-deployment#about-environments)) |

**`prevent_self_review: false` — correctness constraint, not a loosened control.** Solo-maintainer setups cannot self-approve when this is `true`, so the gate would deadlock on any maintainer-triggered publish. The value flips to `true` the moment a second reviewer joins — do not leave it loose by inertia.

**`NPM_TOKEN` is NOT scoped to this environment.** It remains at repo-level secrets during the OIDC transition and is removed entirely post-v1.0.0 (see Story 6.3). The invariant: the `release` env must have zero secrets. If a future change scopes any secret here, re-audit whether the OIDC trusted-publisher path is still in force.

**Coupling with npm trusted publishing (Story 1.3).** The npm trusted publisher binds on four fields — `organization=armelhbobdad`, `repository=bmad-module-skill-forge`, `workflow filename=release.yaml`, `environment=release`. The environment name above is load-bearing: any rename here must be accompanied by a matching npm-side update in the same change, or the next publish returns 404.

### Inspect current state

```bash
# Environment itself (reviewers, branch-policy shape, prevent_self_review)
gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release

# Allowed deployment branches (expect: one entry, name "main")
gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies

# Environment-scoped secrets (expect: zero)
gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/secrets
```

### Restore / re-apply

Two-call pattern — the environment and its branch-policy list are separate resources. Creating the environment alone with `custom_branch_policies: true` leaves the allow-list empty, which rejects every dispatch; the second call is mandatory.

```bash
# (1) Create or update the environment with the required-reviewer gate
gh api --method PUT repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release --input - <<'JSON'
{
  "wait_timer": 0,
  "prevent_self_review": false,
  "reviewers": [{ "type": "User", "id": 132626034 }],
  "deployment_branch_policy": {
    "protected_branches": false,
    "custom_branch_policies": true
  }
}
JSON

# (2) Add main to the allowed deployment branches.
#     Returns HTTP 422 ("already_exists") if main is already on the list —
#     safe to ignore on re-apply. To pre-check:
#     gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies
gh api --method POST repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies --input - <<'JSON'
{ "name": "main" }
JSON
```

### Temporarily allowing a feature branch

A release workflow that declares `environment: release` will be rejected from any branch not on the allow-list. For legitimate validation cuts from a feature branch (Story 3.2's alpha cut is the canonical case), widen the allow-list for the duration of the test and tighten it back immediately:

```bash
# Allow the feature branch and capture the returned policy id into a shell var.
# Using command substitution + --jq .id avoids the "eyeball the JSON and paste
# the id later" footgun — if the POST succeeds, $POLICY_ID is ready for the revoke.
POLICY_ID=$(gh api --method POST \
  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies \
  --input - --jq .id <<'JSON'
{ "name": "feat/my-validation-branch" }
JSON
)
echo "POLICY_ID=$POLICY_ID"  # confirm a numeric id was captured before proceeding

# ... run the validation cut, approve via the reviewer UI, confirm publish ...

# Revoke immediately — the allow-list must return to { main } before leaving the session.
# Quote the expansion so the shell never interprets the id as a redirection token.
gh api --method DELETE \
  "repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies/$POLICY_ID"
```

Leaving a feature branch on the allow-list is an unguarded hole — any later `workflow_dispatch` from that branch would be able to reach the publish path. Treat the revoke as the last step of the validation, not a follow-up.

## npm Trusted Publisher

The future `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1) publishes to npm via **OIDC trusted publishing** — no `NPM_TOKEN` is consulted during the publish step, and every published version carries an auto-attached SLSA Build Level 2 provenance attestation. For this to work, the npm package `bmad-module-skill-forge` has a trusted-publisher entry on npmjs.com that binds on four fields exactly matching what the workflow asserts at run time. A mismatch on any field causes an opaque `404` at publish time — the error ("npm could not match your workflow run") surfaces the failure class but does not name which of the four fields is wrong.

**Registered:** 2026-04-20 by `armelhbobdad`.

| Field             | Value                     |
| ----------------- | ------------------------- |
| Publisher type    | `GitHub Actions`          |
| Organization/user | `armelhbobdad`            |
| Repository        | `bmad-module-skill-forge` |
| Workflow filename | `release.yaml`            |
| Environment       | `release`                 |

**Inspect current state:** visit the [npm package settings for `bmad-module-skill-forge`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge) → **Settings** tab → **Trusted Publisher** section (npm UI as of 2026-04-20; if the tab is reorganised later, the section still lives on the package Settings page). Modification requires 2FA re-entry on the maintainer account. No CLI or public API for programmatic inspection of Trusted Publisher state exists as of 2026-04-20 — drift detection is UI-only until npm exposes one.

**Case-sensitive.** All four fields above use the exact lowercase forms shown; npm's matcher is an exact-string comparison. Do not capitalize on re-registration even if GitHub's UI surfaces a display-form with capitals.

**Rename coupling — the four fields are load-bearing.** Renaming OR deleting the `release` GitHub environment (see § Release Environment), renaming or moving `release.yaml` within `.github/workflows/`, or flipping the extension between `.yaml` and `.yml` each require matching updates in the same PR to: (a) the npm-side Trusted Publisher, (b) the Registered table above, and (c) the `## Release Process` enumeration in `README.md` (which names both `release` and the Trusted Publisher). Skipping any of these produces an opaque `404` on the next publish — the error names the failure class, not the specific field.

**Pre-registration inversion.** This entry was registered **before** `release.yaml` was authored (Story 3.1). The first live validator of the full OIDC chain is Story 3.2's alpha cut. If that cut's publish step 404s, open a **three-way comparison**: (1) the npm Settings tab, (2) the workflow YAML's `name` / `on` / `jobs.<id>.environment` lines, and (3) the Registered table above. The table is the ground truth because it captured the values at npm-save time — compare both the npm record and the workflow header against the table, never the workflow against itself (verifying the workflow against its own header will silently confirm a typo).

**`NPM_TOKEN` is a legacy residual, not a safety net.** The token remains at repo-level secrets for two reasons: (a) the legacy `publish.yaml` still uses it until Story 3.3 retires that path, and (b) Story 6.3 deletes the secret entirely post-v1.0.0. Treat its continued presence as attack surface to minimize, not defence-in-depth — a repo-scope token is reachable from any workflow with `secrets.*` access. The token does NOT sit "behind" OIDC: `release.yaml` does not yet exist, so there is no OIDC path for it to be a fallback to. If a future OIDC incident forces a last-resort token-based re-publish, document the flip in the commit body and revert as soon as OIDC is restored.

**Fixing a bad registration.** The npm UI exposes both **Edit** and **Delete** on an existing Trusted Publisher entry (observed 2026-04-20). Prefer edit for a single-field typo; prefer delete-and-re-add if multiple fields are wrong or the edit form ever feels ambiguous. **Pre-Story 3.2**: there is no destructive side effect because no publish is attempted yet, and delete-and-re-add keeps the audit trail cleaner. **Post-Story 3.2**: a publish that fires during the delete-and-re-add window will 404 — gate any delete-and-re-add behind a manual publish freeze (pause any active `release.yaml` runs, confirm no tags are in-flight) before touching the entry.

<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->

## Rollback Playbook

_To be authored in Story 4.1 — rollback playbook covering scenarios such as failed publish, tag mismatch, bad `latest` dist-tag, OIDC auth failure, and post-publish security issue._

file.content:
# markdownlint-cli2 configuration
# https://github.com/DavidAnson/markdownlint-cli2

ignores:
  - node_modules/**
  - test/fixtures/**
  - CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md
  - _bmad/**
  - _bmad*/**
  - .*/**
  - z*/**
  - temp/**
  - website/**
  - build/**
  - skills/reports/**

# Rule configuration
config:
  # Disable all rules by default
  default: false

  # Heading levels should increment by one (h1 -> h2 -> h3, not h1 -> h3)
  MD001: true

  # Duplicate sibling headings (same heading text at same level under same parent)
  MD024:
    siblings_only: true

  # Trailing commas in headings (likely typos)
  MD026:
    punctuation: ","

  # Bare URLs - may not render as links in all parsers
  # Should use <url> or [text](url) format
  MD034: true

  # Spaces inside emphasis markers - breaks rendering
  # e.g., "* text *" won't render as emphasis
  MD037: true

content.content:
100	
101	**(H8)** One-time hand-bump path `0.10.0 → 1.0.0-rc.0` before `version_bump: rc` for Story 5.2. `npm version prerelease --preid=rc` on `0.10.0` produces `0.10.1-rc.0`, not `1.0.0-rc.0`. Procedure: `npm version 1.0.0-rc.0 --no-git-tag-version` on a pre-cut commit to `main` via PR, then dispatch `release.yaml` with `version_bump: rc` to advance to `1.0.0-rc.1`. Story 5.1 documents this in `docs/RELEASING.md § Cutting v1.0.0-rc.1`.
102	
103	**(H9)** `prevent_self_review: false → true` flip on the `release` environment when a second reviewer is onboarded. Epic 1 standing flag; now clarified per Story 3.3 AC #6 drift — this is about *ruleset bypass-list revoke* (remove `RepositoryRole 5, mode: pull_request` bypass) in addition to the env-level flag flip. Full solo-maintainer-workflow-to-multi-reviewer transition needs both.
104	
105	### CI-as-enforced-gate candidates (standalone story post-v1.0.0 or Story 5.1)
106	
107	Carry-over from Epic 1 + Epic 2 retros — prose-only constraints that CI could enforce:
108	
109	**(I1)** `lychee CHANGELOG.md` or `markdown-link-check` CI step scoped to the changelog — regression guard against Story 2.2's `closes [#F59E0B]` class-of-bug.
110	
111	**(I2)** `release` env zero-secrets assertion — `gh api /environments/release/secrets` must return `total_count: 0`, fail CI otherwise.
112	
113	**(I3)** Name-based lookup instead of hard-coded ruleset `13855503` / environment `14347249917` IDs in `docs/RELEASING.md`.
114	
115	**(I4)** Trusted Publisher registration shape vs repo reality assertion — CI reads `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher` table and verifies `.github/workflows/release.yaml` exists, environment `release` exists, `package.json` name matches.
116	
117	**(I5)** `package.json.devDependencies` mirror-entry drift guard in `package-lock.json.packages[""].devDependencies` — lightweight hygiene (Epic 2 carry-over).
118	
119	### No Story 4.2 / Story 3.3 overlap cleanup needed
120	
121	Story 3.3 Patch B shipped the DEPRECATED comment header wording on both `publish.yaml` + `manual-release.yaml` verbatim matching Story 4.2's AC reference text. Story 4.2 scope narrows to CONTRIBUTING.md "Releasing" section prose + `release:*` script fail-loud prefixes in `package.json`. No re-edit of workflow headers needed.
122	
123	## Commitments
124	
125	### Carry-forward into Epic 4
126	
127	1. **Story 4.1 `create-story` MUST import `deferred-work.md § Story 3.1/3.2/3.3 code review` entries for Scenarios D/E/F.** Scenario E (npm publish succeeded but tag/push failed) has a concrete answer from Story 3.2's observed ordering (tag pushed before publish — the risk is inverted from the AC's premise). Scenario D (tag exists but publish failed) maps directly to Story 3.2's orphaned-commit pattern. Scenario F (OIDC compromise) references Story 1.3's TP registration surface.
128	2. **Story 4.1 MUST add a "release.yaml disabled / reverted" scenario** per Story 3.3 code-review defer. Low probability, high-consequence fallback gap when `release.yaml` is the single release root.
129	3. **Story 4.2 `create-story` MUST NOT re-edit the legacy workflow headers** — Story 3.3 Patch B already landed them verbatim. Scope narrows to CONTRIBUTING.md + `package.json release:*` prefixes.
130	4. **Story 4.1 MUST use name-based lookups** (`gh api .../rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id'`) NOT hard-coded ruleset/env IDs in any new prose — Epic 1 code-review defer carries over.
131	
132	### Process improvements (continue across Epic 4+)
133	
134	5. **Live `gh api` / UI recon block at Task 1 of every story.** 9-for-9 across Epics 1+2+3. Unchanged.
135	6. **Adversarial code review on every story, with explicit Patch / Defer / Dismiss triage.** 9-for-9. Unchanged. Post-merge code-review-on-fresh-context + follow-up hardening PR is now a validated pattern (PR #194).
136	7. **When a downstream story's `Given` premise breaks mid-epic, patch the downstream planning artifact in the current story's review.** Epic 2 pattern; held in Epic 3 (Story 3.1 review → Story 3.3 scope; Story 3.2 HIGH → Story 3.3 Patch C).
137	8. **NEW: ACs that contain inline scripts (awk, sed, bash pipelines) MUST be dry-run at story-authoring time, not only at dev-agent time.** Story 3.3 Patch C awk was literally wrong in the spec; dev-agent local dry-run caught it. If it had gone direct to CI on the first `main`-branch release cut, a malformed CHANGELOG would have shipped to npm in v1.0.0's permanent history.
138	9. **NEW: Task 1 recon probes MUST verify AC-encoded claims about API semantics, check-names, ruleset IDs, and env behavior against live state.** Story 3.3 AC #5 and AC #6 both drifted; recon would have surfaced both pre-execution.
139	10. **NEW: Never write `AC #N` in commit bodies.** Use `AC N`, `AC-N`, or `acceptance criterion N`. The `conventionalcommits` preset interprets any `#N` as a GitHub issue reference and emits `closes [#N]` links that pollute the immutable CHANGELOG entry.
140	11. **NEW: `HUSKY: "0"` at job-level env** is the pattern for any GitHub Actions workflow whose bot commits land on runners without the maintainer's local `entire` tool. Pair with (F4) defense-in-depth (`-c core.hooksPath=/dev/null`) if the env is ever split across matrix cells or reusable workflows.
141	
142	### Standing flags (no fire date)
143	
144	12. **Flip `prevent_self_review: false → true` on the `release` environment AND revoke the ruleset's solo-maintainer bypass-list entry** (`RepositoryRole 5, mode: pull_request`) when a second reviewer is onboarded. Epic 1 carry-forward, now clarified per Story 3.3 AC #6 drift — both the env flag and the ruleset bypass need to flip together, not just one.
145	13. **`install.js:23` silent exit 0 on `{success: false}`** contradicts STABILITY.md. Deferred to Story 5.1 pre-flight; revisit if any pre-v1.0.0 work touches that file.
146	14. **`package.json.main` points at side-effectful CLI entrypoint.** Deferred to Story 5.1 pre-flight.

file.content:
**(H8)** One-time hand-bump path `0.10.0 → 1.0.0-rc.0` before `version_bump: rc` for Story 5.2. `npm version prerelease --preid=rc` on `0.10.0` produces `0.10.1-rc.0`, not `1.0.0-rc.0`. Procedure: `npm version 1.0.0-rc.0 --no-git-tag-version` on a pre-cut commit to `main` via PR, then dispatch `release.yaml` with `version_bump: rc` to advance to `1.0.0-rc.1`. Story 5.1 documents this in `docs/RELEASING.md § Cutting v1.0.0-rc.1`.

**(H9)** `prevent_self_review: false → true` flip on the `release` environment when a second reviewer is onboarded. Epic 1 standing flag; now clarified per Story 3.3 AC #6 drift — this is about *ruleset bypass-list revoke* (remove `RepositoryRole 5, mode: pull_request` bypass) in addition to the env-level flag flip. Full solo-maintainer-workflow-to-multi-reviewer transition needs both.

### CI-as-enforced-gate candidates (standalone story post-v1.0.0 or Story 5.1)

Carry-over from Epic 1 + Epic 2 retros — prose-only constraints that CI could enforce:

**(I1)** `lychee CHANGELOG.md` or `markdown-link-check` CI step scoped to the changelog — regression guard against Story 2.2's `closes [#F59E0B]` class-of-bug.

**(I2)** `release` env zero-secrets assertion — `gh api /environments/release/secrets` must return `total_count: 0`, fail CI otherwise.

**(I3)** Name-based lookup instead of hard-coded ruleset `13855503` / environment `14347249917` IDs in `docs/RELEASING.md`.

**(I4)** Trusted Publisher registration shape vs repo reality assertion — CI reads `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher` table and verifies `.github/workflows/release.yaml` exists, environment `release` exists, `package.json` name matches.

**(I5)** `package.json.devDependencies` mirror-entry drift guard in `package-lock.json.packages[""].devDependencies` — lightweight hygiene (Epic 2 carry-over).

### No Story 4.2 / Story 3.3 overlap cleanup needed

Story 3.3 Patch B shipped the DEPRECATED comment header wording on both `publish.yaml` + `manual-release.yaml` verbatim matching Story 4.2's AC reference text. Story 4.2 scope narrows to CONTRIBUTING.md "Releasing" section prose + `release:*` script fail-loud prefixes in `package.json`. No re-edit of workflow headers needed.

## Commitments

### Carry-forward into Epic 4

1. **Story 4.1 `create-story` MUST import `deferred-work.md § Story 3.1/3.2/3.3 code review` entries for Scenarios D/E/F.** Scenario E (npm publish succeeded but tag/push failed) has a concrete answer from Story 3.2's observed ordering (tag pushed before publish — the risk is inverted from the AC's premise). Scenario D (tag exists but publish failed) maps directly to Story 3.2's orphaned-commit pattern. Scenario F (OIDC compromise) references Story 1.3's TP registration surface.
2. **Story 4.1 MUST add a "release.yaml disabled / reverted" scenario** per Story 3.3 code-review defer. Low probability, high-consequence fallback gap when `release.yaml` is the single release root.
3. **Story 4.2 `create-story` MUST NOT re-edit the legacy workflow headers** — Story 3.3 Patch B already landed them verbatim. Scope narrows to CONTRIBUTING.md + `package.json release:*` prefixes.
4. **Story 4.1 MUST use name-based lookups** (`gh api .../rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id'`) NOT hard-coded ruleset/env IDs in any new prose — Epic 1 code-review defer carries over.

### Process improvements (continue across Epic 4+)

5. **Live `gh api` / UI recon block at Task 1 of every story.** 9-for-9 across Epics 1+2+3. Unchanged.
6. **Adversarial code review on every story, with explicit Patch / Defer / Dismiss triage.** 9-for-9. Unchanged. Post-merge code-review-on-fresh-context + follow-up hardening PR is now a validated pattern (PR #194).
7. **When a downstream story's `Given` premise breaks mid-epic, patch the downstream planning artifact in the current story's review.** Epic 2 pattern; held in Epic 3 (Story 3.1 review → Story 3.3 scope; Story 3.2 HIGH → Story 3.3 Patch C).
8. **NEW: ACs that contain inline scripts (awk, sed, bash pipelines) MUST be dry-run at story-authoring time, not only at dev-agent time.** Story 3.3 Patch C awk was literally wrong in the spec; dev-agent local dry-run caught it. If it had gone direct to CI on the first `main`-branch release cut, a malformed CHANGELOG would have shipped to npm in v1.0.0's permanent history.
9. **NEW: Task 1 recon probes MUST verify AC-encoded claims about API semantics, check-names, ruleset IDs, and env behavior against live state.** Story 3.3 AC #5 and AC #6 both drifted; recon would have surfaced both pre-execution.
10. **NEW: Never write `AC #N` in commit bodies.** Use `AC N`, `AC-N`, or `acceptance criterion N`. The `conventionalcommits` preset interprets any `#N` as a GitHub issue reference and emits `closes [#N]` links that pollute the immutable CHANGELOG entry.
11. **NEW: `HUSKY: "0"` at job-level env** is the pattern for any GitHub Actions workflow whose bot commits land on runners without the maintainer's local `entire` tool. Pair with (F4) defense-in-depth (`-c core.hooksPath=/dev/null`) if the env is ever split across matrix cells or reusable workflows.

### Standing flags (no fire date)

12. **Flip `prevent_self_review: false → true` on the `release` environment AND revoke the ruleset's solo-maintainer bypass-list entry** (`RepositoryRole 5, mode: pull_request`) when a second reviewer is onboarded. Epic 1 carry-forward, now clarified per Story 3.3 AC #6 drift — both the env flag and the ruleset bypass need to flip together, not just one.
13. **`install.js:23` silent exit 0 on `{success: false}`** contradicts STABILITY.md. Deferred to Story 5.1 pre-flight; revisit if any pre-v1.0.0 work touches that file.
14. **`package.json.main` points at side-effectful CLI entrypoint.** Deferred to Story 5.1 pre-flight.
15. **Orphaned commit `2a57dcbd` (Story 3.2 alpha-cut CI-ephemeral) is reachable only via tag `v0.10.1-alpha.0` + branch `feat/release-workflow`.** Do NOT delete the branch until Story 5.4 closes (v1.0.0 shipped) AND GitHub retention behavior on orphaned commits is confirmed. Story 6-adjacent cleanup story is the natural home for branch deletion.

## Readiness assessment

| Dimension | Status |
|-----------|--------|
| Story completion | ✅ 3/3 done |
| Live artifacts captured | ✅ commits `1295bf6`/`34ad130`, `cbc7851` + `de2334b` + `cae5861` + `535e38f`, `ab74c10` + `76a62cd` + `10c1134` + `6a6a521`; merge-commits `14cedfe` (PR #193) + `b662bce` (PR #194) |
| Alpha cut live on npm | ✅ `bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1-alpha.0` under `--tag alpha` with SLSA-L2 provenance verified |
| `release.yaml` on `main` | ✅ 254 lines; canonical OIDC path |
| Legacy workflows inert | ✅ `publish.yaml` `v*` trigger neutralized; both have DEPRECATED headers |
| CHANGELOG preamble-restore hardened | ✅ 4 code-review guards landed in PR #194 |
| Adversarial code review | ✅ all three stories + post-merge hardening loop |
| Deferred work tracked | ✅ `deferred-work.md` current (~30 items across Epics 1–3) |
| Unresolved blockers for Epic 4 start | none — F1–F6, G1–G4, H1–H9, I1–I5 are inputs for Story 5.1 audit or post-v1.0.0, NOT blockers for Story 4.1/4.2 |
| Scope envelope adherence | ✅ 3/3 stories held to file-list AC |
| Feature branch preservation | ✅ `feat/release-workflow` on origin at `6a6a521` (NFR12 reversibility boundary intact) |
| Epic status | already flipped `in-progress` → `done` at Story 3.3 close-out |

Epic 3 is truly done. The canonical OIDC release path is on `main`, operationally proven via a live alpha cut with verifiable SLSA-L2 provenance. Epic 4 (docs-only: rollback playbook + deprecation prose) can start whenever.

## Next steps

1. Epic 4 planning via `bmad-create-story` for Story 4.1 when ready — import commitments 1–4 above into Dev Notes at create-story phase.
2. Story 4.2 scope confirmed narrowed (no workflow-header re-edit).
3. Story 5.1 pre-v1.0.0 audit inherits items H1–H9, plus decide the disposition of F1–F6 and G1–G4 at audit time.
4. No epic-update review session needed — nothing in Epic 3 invalidates Epic 4, Epic 5, or Epic 6's current definitions. Epic 4 is still doc-only per `epics.md`; workflow hardening remains queued for Story 5.1 or post-v1.0.0 per the original sequencing.

66:NFR3: **Rollback frequency** — post-v1.0.0, `npm deprecate` invocations SHALL occur fewer than once per quarter.
70:NFR5: **Audit-trail completeness** — 100% of releases SHALL have a corresponding GitHub Release page + `CHANGELOG.md` entry.
72:NFR6: **Version immutability (policy)** — once `v1.0.0` is published under `--tag latest`, it SHALL NEVER be unpublished or republished; rollback SHALL use `npm deprecate` + ship forward.
74:NFR7: **Minimum security bar** — v1.0.0 SHALL require: OIDC Trusted Publishing, branch protection on `main` (required PR reviews, required status checks including `quality.yaml`, no force-push), `release` environment protection rule with required reviewer.
80:NFR10: **Commit-trail auditability** — every release SHALL trace back to an auditable chain of conventional commits; release notes categorization SHALL be deterministic.
84:NFR12: **Reversibility during migration** — each of the 7 migration steps SHALL be independently reversible until the next step starts (no irreversible side-effects mid-migration).
138:| NFR3 (rollback frequency) | Epic 4 (playbook), Epic 5/6 (post-ship metric) | Target: <1 per quarter post-v1.0.0 |
140:| NFR5 (audit-trail 100%) | Epic 2 (CHANGELOG exists), Epic 3 (mechanism) | GitHub Release + CHANGELOG entry per release |
141:| NFR6 (v1.0.0 immutability) | Epic 4 (policy), Epic 5 (operationalized) | Never unpublish v1.0.0 |
142:| NFR7 (min security bar) | Epic 1 | Branch protection + env + required reviews/checks |
145:| NFR10 (commit-trail auditability) | Epic 3 | Conventional commits → deterministic categorization |
147:| NFR12 (migration reversibility) | Epic 3 + Epic 4 (documented) | Each step independently reversible |
155:**NFRs covered:** NFR7 (min security bar), NFR11 (free under public-repo pricing)
161:**NFRs covered:** NFR5 (audit-trail mechanism — CHANGELOG half)
167:**NFRs covered:** NFR1 (design), NFR2 (design), NFR4 (provenance 100%), NFR5 (mechanism), NFR8 (runtime floor), NFR10 (auditable trail), NFR12 (reversibility during migration)
173:**NFRs covered:** NFR3 (rollback frequency — playbook makes target achievable), NFR6 (v1.0.0 immutability policy documented), NFR12 (reversibility documented)
179:**NFRs covered:** NFR6 (v1.0.0 immutability operationalized), NFR9 (cross-platform verification within 1h), NFR1/NFR2 first-measurement
185:**NFRs covered:** NFR2/NFR3 (continued measurement)
465:**And** the feature-branch commits SHALL be preserved (do NOT force-push or reset after a published tag — NFR12 reversibility boundary)

145:| NFR10 (commit-trail auditability) | Epic 3 | Conventional commits → deterministic categorization |
147:| NFR12 (migration reversibility) | Epic 3 + Epic 4 (documented) | Each step independently reversible |
155:**NFRs covered:** NFR7 (min security bar), NFR11 (free under public-repo pricing)
161:**NFRs covered:** NFR5 (audit-trail mechanism — CHANGELOG half)
167:**NFRs covered:** NFR1 (design), NFR2 (design), NFR4 (provenance 100%), NFR5 (mechanism), NFR8 (runtime floor), NFR10 (auditable trail), NFR12 (reversibility during migration)
173:**NFRs covered:** NFR3 (rollback frequency — playbook makes target achievable), NFR6 (v1.0.0 immutability policy documented), NFR12 (reversibility documented)
179:**NFRs covered:** NFR6 (v1.0.0 immutability operationalized), NFR9 (cross-platform verification within 1h), NFR1/NFR2 first-measurement
185:**NFRs covered:** NFR2/NFR3 (continued measurement)
465:**And** the feature-branch commits SHALL be preserved (do NOT force-push or reset after a published tag — NFR12 reversibility boundary)
541:**When** I review for NFR3/NFR6 alignment
